class_schedule.topic: How Digital Reading Differs from Print Reading
learning_objectives: Explain how screen-based reading behavior differs from print reading behavior
Identify the implications of F-pattern and Z-pattern scanning for content structure
Apply the inverted pyramid structure to digital content
readings: Krug, Don't Make Me Think, Ch. 1-3
Nielsen Norman Group, 'How Users Read on the Web' (seminal study)

lecture_notes.introduction: Writing for digital platforms is fundamentally different from writing for print. People do not read on screens the way they read books. They scan, skip, and leave. Every sentence must earn the reader's continued attention.
key_concepts: F-pattern scanning: eye-tracking research showing how users scan web pages in an F-shaped pattern
Inverted pyramid: leading with the most important information, followed by supporting details, then background
Scannability: using headings, bullet points, bold text, and short paragraphs to support scanning behavior
Front-loading: placing key words and phrases at the beginning of headings, sentences, and paragraphs
The 8-second challenge: capturing attention before the average reader decides to leave or scroll past
lecture_notes.demonstration: Eye-tracking heatmap analysis of three web pages, comparing a wall-of-text page with a well-structured scannable page, measuring reading depth and comprehension
setup_requirements: Web browser for live page analysis

review.value: Eye-tracking research pattern showing users read the first line horizontally, scan down slightly, read a shorter horizontal line, then scan vertically down the left side, meaning content buried on the right or at the bottom is frequently missed
A writing structure placing the most newsworthy or important information first, followed by supporting details, then background context, ensuring readers who leave early still receive the key message

learning_objectives: Write compelling headlines that balance clarity with curiosity
Apply headline formulas appropriate for different platforms and contexts
Test headline effectiveness using A/B testing principles

lecture_notes.introduction: The headline is the most important sentence in any digital content. On social media, it may be the only sentence anyone reads. In email, the subject line determines whether your message is opened or ignored. Headline writing is a skill that repays constant practice.
key_concepts: The 80/20 rule of headlines: 80% of people read the headline, only 20% read the body
Headline formulas: How to [achieve desired outcome], [Number] ways to [solve problem], Why [surprising claim]
Specificity over cleverness: concrete details outperform vague promises
Email subject lines: personalization, urgency (real, not manufactured), length constraints
Platform-specific headline norms: SEO titles vs. social headlines vs. newsletter subjects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing 10 headline variations for a single article, scoring each with CoSchedule Headline Analyzer, and discussing what makes the top-scoring versions more effective
setup_requirements: Access to CoSchedule Headline Analyzer (free version)
Sample articles for headline writing practice

review.value: Headlines with specific numbers, names, or details ('5 Email Subject Lines That Doubled Our Open Rate') outperform vague alternatives ('How to Write Better Subject Lines') because specificity implies credibility and concrete value
A headline technique that opens a gap between what the reader knows and what they want to know, motivating a click, but only effective when the content delivers on the promise

learning_objectives: Adapt writing tone and length for different social platforms
Write effective calls to action for social media posts
Craft social copy that encourages engagement (comments, shares, saves)
readings: Platform-specific character limits and best practices guide (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media copywriting is a discipline of radical compression. Every word must work. Every post must have a purpose. The best social copy feels effortless but is the result of deliberate craft.
key_concepts: Platform voice adaptation: professional on LinkedIn, conversational on Twitter/X, visual-first on Instagram
The hook: the first line that appears before 'see more' determines whether anyone reads the rest
Call to action (CTA) design: specific, low-friction asks that align with the platform's native behaviors
Hashtag strategy: discovery vs. clutter, branded vs. community hashtags
Engagement prompts: questions, polls, fill-in-the-blank, hot takes that invite response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rewriting a single product announcement as platform-native posts for LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Instagram, and TikTok (caption), discussing the adaptations required for each

review.value: The first 125-150 characters of a social media post that appear before the 'see more' truncation on most platforms, serving as the headline that determines whether the full post is read

The amount of effort required to complete a call to action; 'double-tap if you agree' is low friction, 'visit our website and fill out a form' is high friction, and the CTA should match the audience's relationship stage

learning_objectives: Explain how search engines evaluate and rank written content
Integrate target keywords naturally into digital writing without keyword stuffing
Write meta descriptions and title tags that improve click-through rates from search results
readings: Enge et al., The Art of SEO, Ch. 6 - Content for SEO
Google Search Central, 'SEO Starter Guide' (writing-relevant sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Search engine optimization and good writing are not in conflict. The best SEO writing is clear, well-structured, and answers the reader's question thoroughly. Understanding how search engines evaluate content makes you a better digital writer, not a worse one.
key_concepts: Search intent: informational, navigational, transactional, and commercial investigation queries
Keyword research: identifying what your audience actually searches for vs. what you think they search for
Natural keyword integration: placing keywords in headings, first paragraphs, and alt text without awkward phrasing
Meta descriptions: writing 155-character summaries that function as ad copy in search results
Content structure for SEO: headings hierarchy (H1-H3), internal linking, content length considerations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Taking a draft blog post and optimizing it for a target keyword: researching the keyword, analyzing competitor content, restructuring headings, writing a meta description, and adding internal links
setup_requirements: Google Keyword Planner or Ubersuggest (free version)
Sample draft blog post for optimization exercise

review.value: The underlying goal behind a search query: informational (learn something), navigational (find a specific page), transactional (buy something), or commercial (compare options before buying). Content must match intent to rank.
The outdated and penalized practice of unnaturally repeating target keywords to manipulate search rankings; modern SEO rewards natural language, semantic relevance, and comprehensive topic coverage

learning_objectives: Write professional emails that achieve their intended purpose with clarity and appropriate tone
Design newsletter content that balances value delivery with organizational messaging
Apply segmentation principles to tailor email content for different audience groups

lecture_notes.introduction: Email remains the highest-ROI digital communication channel. Despite predictions of its demise, email generates more engagement and conversion than any social platform. Mastering email writing is not optional for digital communicators.
key_concepts: Subject line craft: preview text as a second headline, personalization tokens, length optimization
Email structure: one primary CTA per email, scannable layout, mobile-first design
Newsletter value proposition: why should someone open this every week?
Tone calibration: matching formality to audience expectations and relationship stage
Segmentation logic: sending different content to different groups based on behavior or demographics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Critiquing five real newsletter issues from well-known brands, identifying what makes each effective or ineffective, then drafting a newsletter outline for a campus organization
setup_requirements: Email examples collected during the week (students bring 3 newsletters)
Mailchimp or Substack free account for template exploration

review.value: The snippet of text displayed after the subject line in email inboxes, functioning as a second headline that can double open rates when crafted deliberately rather than left to auto-generate from the email body
The principle that each email should have a single primary call to action; multiple CTAs create decision paralysis and reduce click-through rates by dividing the reader's attention

learning_objectives: Apply plain language principles to make digital writing accessible to broader audiences
Write alt text, captions, and transcripts that make multimedia content accessible
Identify and avoid exclusionary language patterns in digital communications

lecture_notes.introduction: Accessible writing is not dumbed-down writing. It is clear, precise, and structured for comprehension by the widest possible audience, including people using screen readers, people for whom English is a second language, and people with cognitive disabilities.
key_concepts: Plain language principles: short sentences, active voice, common words, logical structure
Readability scoring: Flesch-Kincaid, Gunning Fog; targeting appropriate grade levels for audience
Alt text writing: descriptive, contextual, functional descriptions for images
Caption and transcript standards: accuracy, speaker identification, sound description
Inclusive language: person-first language, gender-inclusive terms, avoiding assumptions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rewriting a jargon-heavy organizational announcement into plain language, then writing alt text for the five images that accompany it, testing both with a screen reader
setup_requirements: Hemingway Editor (free web app) for readability scoring
Screen reader for testing (VoiceOver on Mac, NVDA on Windows)

review.value: A text description of an image that is read by screen readers and displayed when images fail to load; effective alt text describes the content and function of the image in its specific context, not just its visual appearance
A readability formula estimating the U.S. school grade level needed to understand a text; most successful digital content targets grades 6-8, regardless of the actual audience's education level

questions.question: Describe the F-pattern scanning behavior and explain two specific writing techniques you would use to ensure your most important content is seen by readers who scan rather than read.
questions.answer: The F-pattern describes how web readers scan: they read the first horizontal line of text most thoroughly, scan down slightly and read a second (shorter) horizontal line, then scan vertically down the left side of the page. Two techniques: (1) Front-load headings and topic sentences by placing the most important words at the beginning, since readers scanning vertically will see only the left portion. (2) Use bold text and bullet points to create visual anchor points that interrupt the vertical scan and draw the eye to key information even when the reader is not reading line by line.

rubric.key: First technique is relevant and specific
Second technique is relevant and specific

questions.question: Rewrite this weak headline into a stronger version and explain what principles you applied: 'Some Thoughts About Marketing'
questions.answer: Stronger version: '7 Marketing Mistakes That Cost Small Businesses $10,000 a Year.' Principles applied: (1) Specificity: replaced vague 'some thoughts' with a concrete number and dollar amount. (2) Audience targeting: identified 'small businesses' as the reader. (3) Stakes: framed the topic as a costly problem rather than abstract musings. (4) Curiosity gap: the reader wants to know if they are making these mistakes. The revised headline promises concrete, actionable value instead of the vague, passive original.

rubric.key: Identifies specificity as an applied principle

questions.question: Explain search intent and describe why a blog post optimized for the keyword 'buy running shoes' would likely fail even if it contains that exact phrase repeatedly.
questions.answer: Search intent is the underlying goal behind a search query. 'Buy running shoes' has transactional intent: the searcher wants to purchase, not read. A blog post is informational content, which mismatches the transactional intent. Search engines prioritize content that matches intent, so product pages and e-commerce listings would rank instead. Additionally, repeating the exact phrase repeatedly constitutes keyword stuffing, which is penalized by modern search algorithms. The content fails on both intent alignment and optimization technique.

rubric.key: Addresses keyword stuffing as a penalized practice

questions.question: What is the 'one-CTA rule' for email, and why does it improve click-through rates compared to emails with multiple calls to action?
questions.answer: The one-CTA rule states that each email should have a single primary call to action rather than multiple competing asks. It improves click-through rates because multiple CTAs create decision paralysis (the paradox of choice), where readers faced with several options choose none. A single CTA focuses the reader's attention, makes the desired action clear, and reduces the cognitive effort required to take the next step. Every element of the email can be designed to support that one action rather than pulling the reader in different directions.

major_assignments.title: Digital Writing Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Create a professional digital writing portfolio containing original content across five formats: a long-form blog post, a set of social media posts for three platforms, an email newsletter, SEO-optimized web copy, and a crisis communication statement. All pieces should address the same organization or topic to demonstrate adaptability across formats.
objectives: Demonstrate mastery of platform-specific writing conventions
Apply SEO principles naturally within written content
Adapt tone, length, and structure for different digital channels
Write clear, accessible content that meets professional standards
deliverables: Long-form blog post (1000-1200 words) optimized for a target keyword
Social media post set: one post each for LinkedIn, Twitter/X, and Instagram
Email newsletter (one complete issue with subject line and preview text)
SEO web page copy (400-500 words for a service or product page)
Crisis communication statement (200-300 words) for a specified scenario

criteria: Prose is clear, concise, and free of grammatical errors
Tone is appropriate and consistent within each piece
Content provides genuine value to the target audience
Readability is appropriate for the platform and audience
Each piece follows the conventions of its target platform
Length, structure, and formatting match platform norms
CTAs and engagement techniques are platform-appropriate
SEO principles are applied naturally where relevant
All pieces clearly serve the same organization or topic
Brand voice is consistent across all formats
Portfolio demonstrates range while maintaining coherence
Professional writing quality demonstrating command of digital conventions
Effective platform adaptation showing versatility across channels
Strategic coherence tying all pieces to a unified organizational voice
Polished portfolio suitable for professional presentation

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on digital writing concepts and techniques

weekly_writing_exercises.description: Weekly platform-specific writing exercises with peer review

major_assignment.description: Professional digital writing portfolio across five formats

midterm_examination.description: Practical writing examination demonstrating mastery of first-half techniques

final_portfolio_revision.description: Revised and expanded final portfolio incorporating instructor and peer feedback

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Peer review workshops require attendance; missing a peer review without prior arrangement results in zero credit for that exercise.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late writing exercises lose 10% per day. Portfolio components submitted late lose 15% per day since peer review depends on timely delivery.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review is required and collaborative. All submitted writing must be original individual work. Using AI writing tools is permitted only for brainstorming and must be disclosed.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All writing must be original. Plagiarism detection software is used on all submissions. Properly cite all sources. AI-generated text submitted as original work constitutes academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop with internet access, a word processor, and access to Google Docs for peer review. Free accounts on Hemingway Editor, CoSchedule Headline Analyzer, and Mailchimp are required.
